title: What Do I Still Lack?
subtitle: The rich young man kneels before Jesus and asks the way to eternal life

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of your possessions fall silent.
One thing alone is good, essentially and of itself: God.
Come before Him empty, and ask what you still lack.
gospel: And behold, a certain young man came to Jesus,
and bending his knee before Him, he asked Him, saying:
"Good Master, what good shall I do that I may have eternal life?"
And Jesus said to him: "Why callest thou me good?
No one is good but God alone.
But if thou wilt enter into life, keep the commandments."
The young man saith to Him: "Which commandments?"
Jesus said: "Thou shalt not kill, thou shalt not commit adultery,
thou shalt not steal, thou shalt not bear false witness,
honour thy father and thy mother,
and thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself."
The young man saith to Him: "All these have I kept from my youth;
what is yet wanting to me?"
behold: Behold a certain young man drawing near,
and bending his knee before Jesus.
He kneels, and he begs Him, saying:
"Good Master, what good shall I do
that I may receive eternal life?"
He had heard from the Lord that only those like little children
are worthy of the kingdom of God,
and so, desiring to be more certain about entering the kingdom,
he asks — not in parables, but plainly —
by what merits, by what works,
He thought Jesus to be a mere man,
though he believed Him holy, and a good teacher.
And Jesus answered his very thought:
No one is good but the one and only God."
As if to say: since thou believest me a mere man,
thou oughtest not to call me good.
For God alone is good by His nature.
consider: God alone is good — essentially, through Himself, and from Himself.
No other is good except by sharing in Him, and from Him.
Chrysostom says: this does not shut men out from goodness,
but only from comparison with the divine goodness.
"If thou wilt enter into life, keep the commandments."
This is enough for salvation.
What is more than this is not of necessity, but of supererogation.
The commandments are not kept for eternal life
unless they are kept out of charity —
for charity is the end of the commandments.
Who loves his neighbour has fulfilled the Law,
for in the love of neighbour the love of God is understood.
Many today make for themselves strange gods.
As Augustine says: whatever a man loves above all else, that he worships.
The proud man has pride for his god,
the lustful, his lust; the greedy, his greed.
And the young man — he has kept all these from his youth,
which scarcely anyone among the religious would find today.
Yet still he asks: what is yet wanting to me?
One thing remains: to leave all, and follow.
To despise all temporal things in heart,
and to relinquish them in deed.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, grant to me — wretched and frail,
who until now have kept Thy commandments negligently and badly —
grant me at least from now on to keep them,
that with those who keep them I may be worthy to enter into life.
Grant me also, most merciful Lord,
I may despise all temporal things for Thy sake in my heart,
and, having left all things, may follow Thee,
imitating Thee, and walking in Thy footsteps;
that, with Thee as ruler, with Thee as guide,
I may come rejoicing to Thee —
and forever delight in the sight of Thee. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Nemo enim bonus, nisi solus et unus Deus.
latinJewel.english: For no one is good, except the one and only God.
